justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified as a domain model change — bounded_context analysis calls out 'merging concerns that were deliberately separated'; (2) Data ownership question raised — BR-4 establishes Warehouse as sole authority, asks who owns the blocking decision; (3) TOCTOU race condition thoroughly identified — BR-3, BR-6, AC-3, and rejection_reasons all address concurrent order races with testable assertions; (4) Stock reservation vs check distinction surfaced — 'Stock Reservation' flagged as a MISSING CONCEPT in ubiquitous language, BR-6 requires atomic check-and-reserve; (5) Escalation to MJ implicit via rejection_reasons and architectural escalations about integration patterns; (6) New cross-context invariant stated in BR-3 and BR-6.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough TOCTOU analysis — not just identified but made testable with AC-3's concurrent order scenario and BR-6's 100-concurrent-orders assertion
Stock Reservation correctly identified as a MISSING CONCEPT that neither context currently owns, with clear escalation
Data ownership cleanly stated in BR-4 with rejection_reason reinforcing that direct cross-context reads violate context autonomy
Edge cases go beyond the rubric — reservation TTL, cancelled order releasing reservations, Warehouse unavailability fallback are all surfaced
Escalations are well-structured with concrete options and recommendations, covering partial-order policy, blocked-order lifecycle, real-time SLA definition, and failover
Low confidence score (45) is well-calibrated given the fundamental ambiguities identified

MJ escalation is implicit via architectural concerns in escalations and rejection_reasons rather than an explicit 'escalate to MJ' callout, though the architectural coupling pattern questions are clearly flagged
